m_Item1.QuestionText: In which type of loop is the 'break' statement most commonly used to exit early?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement can be used in 'for', 'while', and 'do-while' loops to exit the loop prematurely.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'break' statement do in a loop?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement is used to exit the loop entirely, transferring control to the statement following the loop.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen when a 'break' statement is used inside a nested loop?

Options.OptionText: It breaks out of the outer loop
It breaks out of the innermost loop

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement breaks out of the innermost loop in which it is used.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where can the 'break' statement be used?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement is used to exit a loop or switch statement completely.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main difference between 'break' and 'continue' statements in loops?

Options.OptionText: 'break' exits the loop; 'continue' skips an iteration
'continue' exits the loop; 'break' skips an iteration

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' exits the loop entirely, while 'continue' skips the current iteration and continues with the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scenario is 'break' statement most suitable for?

Options.OptionText: To skip code execution within a loop
To exit a loop when a certain condition is met
To check all elements in a loop
To initialize variables in a loop

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' is used when a condition is met, and exiting the loop is necessary.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code snippet?
for(int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if(i == 3) break;
    printf("%d", i);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks when i is equal to 3, so 0, 1, and 2 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which section of a switch-case block is the 'break' statement usually found?

Options.OptionText: At the end of the entire switch block

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' is used inside each case to terminate the case and prevent fall-through to subsequent cases.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a loop runs indefinitely, which statement can you use to eventually stop it?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement can terminate an infinite loop at a specified condition.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can the 'break' statement be used to exit from multiple levels of nested loops?

Options.OptionText: No, it exits only the innermost loop
No, it is not allowed in nested loops

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement only exits the innermost loop or switch statement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What role does 'break' play in preventing infinite loops?

Options.OptionText: It exits the loop once a condition is met

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' can be used within a conditional statement to exit loops that may otherwise run indefinitely.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword can be considered as the opposite of 'break' in terms of loop control?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'continue' statement skips the remaining code inside the current iteration and moves control back to the loop's top for the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following code print?
int i = 0;
while(1) {
    if(i == 3) break;
    i++;
}
printf("%d", i);
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks when i is 3, and thus 3 is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which part of the for loop does 'break' immediately affect?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' affects the execution flow by terminating the loop body directly.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the outcome of the code snippet?
int i = 0;
do {
    printf("%d", i);
    if(i == 2) break;
} while(++i < 5);
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement exits the loop when i is 2, so 0, 1, and 2 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can 'break' be used in an 'if' statement?

Options.OptionText: Yes, if within a loop or switch
Yes, but it will cause a compile error

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' can be used within an 'if' statement only if the 'if' is inside a loop or switch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does a 'break' statement affect a 'switch' block?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement exits the switch block, transferring control to the code following the switch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When optimizing loops, under what condition is using 'break' helpful?

Options.OptionText: To exit early once a specific condition is satisfied

m_Item1.Explanation: 'break' allows a loop to exit as soon as a necessary condition is met, which can make loops more efficient.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following terminates of a switch-case statement execution immediately?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'break' statement is used to terminate a switch-case execution immediately and passes control to the next statement outside of the switch.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following C code?
int i;
for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if (i == 3) break;
    printf("%d ", i);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks when i is 3, so only 0, 1, and 2 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a nested loop, the break statement will terminate:
m_Item1.Explanation: The break statement terminates only the loop in which it is placed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if you use the continue statement in a do-while loop?

Options.OptionText: The next iteration of the loop will start
The program will go into an infinite loop
It will cause a compilation error

m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement will skip the remaining code in the current iteration and forcedly start the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code snippet:
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if (i == 2) continue;
    printf("%d ", i);
}
What will be the output of this code?
m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement skips the iteration when i equals 2, so 2 is not printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following C program?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        if (i == 4) continue;
        printf("%d ", i);
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The number 4 is skipped due to the continue statement, so only 0, 1, 2, and 3 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens when a continue statement is encountered in a for loop?

Options.OptionText: The iteration restarts after executing the condition update
It exits the loop and continues the program execution

m_Item1.Explanation: In a for loop, continue skips the rest of the code in the current iteration, proceeds with condition update, and then reevaluates the loop condition.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of loops, what is the purpose of using a break statement?

Options.OptionText: To skip a specific iteration in the loop
To exit the loop when a certain condition is met
To pause the loop execution temporarily

m_Item1.Explanation: The break statement is used to exit the loop immediately when a specified condition is met.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of this program?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, sum = 0;
    for (i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
        if (i % 2 == 0)
            continue;
        sum += i;
    }
    printf("%d", sum);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement skips even numbers, so the sum of 1, 3, and 5, which is 9, is calculated.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following C program?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 3; ++i) {
        if (i == 1)
            break;
        printf("Loop");
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks immediately after the first 'Loop' is printed when i equals 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In the following C code, which statement will execute if x is 5?
int x = 5;
while (x > 0) {
    x--;
    if (x == 3)
        continue;
    printf("%d ", x);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The value 3 is skipped because of the continue statement, so 4, 2, 1, and 0 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code snippet:
int i = 0;
do {
    if (i == 4)
        break;
    printf("%d ", i);
    i++;
} while (i < 6);
What is the output of this code?
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks before i gets to 4, so only 0, 1, 2, and 3 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this C code?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i = 0;
    while (i < 4) {
        i++;
        if (i == 2)
            continue;
        printf("%d ", i);
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement skips printing for i=2, so only 1, 3, and 4 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of loops, what is true about the continue statement?

Options.OptionText: It skips the current iteration of the loop
It skips all remaining iterations of the loop

m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves on to the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Examine the following code snippet:
for (int i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
    if (i == 4) break;
    printf("%d ", i);
}
What will be the output?
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks before it can print 4, so the output is 0 1 2 3.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
How many times will 'Hello' be printed by this code?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i = 0;
    while (i < 5) {
        if (i == 3)
            break;
        printf("Hello ");
        i++;
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop breaks after printing 'Hello' for i equal to 0, 1, and 2.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code:
int i;
for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    if (i == 1)
        continue;
    printf("%d ", i);
}
What is the output?
m_Item1.Explanation: When i is 1, the continue statement causes the loop to skip printing, so 0, 2, and 3 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of a break statement in loops?

Options.OptionText: To continue the loop from the next iteration
To print the current loop counter
To skip the rest of the code inside the loop

m_Item1.Explanation: The break statement is used to exit a loop immediately.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does a break statement differ from a return statement?

Options.OptionText: Break exits loops, return exits functions
Break skips one iteration, return skips the entire function
There is no difference in behavior

m_Item1.Explanation: The break statement exits loops, while the return statement exits functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which situation is a continue statement more useful than a break statement?

Options.OptionText: When you want to terminate the loop early
When you want to skip processing a specific iteration
When you want to exit the current function
When you want to loop over certain elements again

m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement is more useful when you want to skip the rest of the current iteration but continue executing the loop with the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When should you avoid using the break statement in your code?

Options.OptionText: If it disrupts the flow of logic

m_Item1.Explanation: Avoid using the break statement when it disrupts logical flow and makes code harder to read and maintain.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of executing this code?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int x = 0;
    while (x < 10) {
        if (x == 5) break;
        printf("%d ", x);
        x++;
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop exits when x equals 5, therefore it prints 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can the use of break and continue enhance loop control?

Options.OptionText: By allowing nested loops to run in reverse
By managing when and where to exit or skip iterations in a loop
By automatically sorting data within a loop

m_Item1.Explanation: Break and continue provide additional control over loop execution, managing when to terminate or skip iterations.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will this code output and why?
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        if (i == 3)
            continue;
        printf("%d ", i);
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Error because of incorrect use of continue

m_Item1.Explanation: The number 3 is skipped due to the continue statement, so 0, 1, 2, and 4 are printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about break and continue is true?

Options.OptionText: Break can only be used in for loops
Continue can only be used in while loops
Both can be used in any loop statements

m_Item1.Explanation: Both break and continue can be used in any loop structure like for, while, and do-while.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a while loop, which statement can immediately go to the next iteration skipping remaining code?
m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement skips the rest of the code and jumps directly to the next iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of this code snippet?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 2; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < 2; j++) {
            if (i == j) break;
            printf("%d%d ", i, j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The loop prints pairs where i != j. When i == j, the inner loop breaks, so it only prints '10'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What does the following code snippet accomplish?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
            if (i == j) {
                printf("* ");
            } else {
                printf("0 ");
            }
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Prints only stars on first row

m_Item1.Explanation: The code prints * where row index equals column index, forming a diagonal on a 3x3 grid.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be printed by the following code?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 2; i++) {
        for (j = 2; j >= 0; j--) {
            if(j == i) break;
            printf("%d%d ", i, j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Each inner loop breaks when j equals i. The outputs are from conditions where j is greater than i.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
How many times does the print statement execute in the following nested loop?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i <= 2; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            printf("%d", j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The inner loop executes 1 time for i=1 and 2 times for i=2, totaling 3 executions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following best describes the output pattern of this program?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
            printf("%d ", i * j);
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Only prints the last row multiples
Prints the index of rows and columns

m_Item1.Explanation: The program prints a 3x3 multiplication table where each element is the product of its row and column indices.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Determine the number of total iterations in the nested loop.
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j, count = 0;
    for (i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= 4; j++) {
            count++;
        }
    }
    printf("%d", count);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The outer loop runs 3 times with the inner loop running 4 times for each iteration, totaling 3 * 4 = 12.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the pattern of stars for the given code?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 5; i > 0; i--) {
        for (j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            printf("*");
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: An increasing number of stars without newline

m_Item1.Explanation: The loop prints lines of stars decreasing in length, forming a reverse right-angle triangle.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What function does a continue statement serve in this nested loop construct?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < 4; j++) {
            if (j == 2) continue;
            printf("%d%d ", i, j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Skips the rest of the current iteration

m_Item1.Explanation: The continue statement causes the loop to skip the rest of the current iteration whenever j equals 2.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this code snippet?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    char i, j;
    for (i = 'A'; i <= 'C'; i++) {
        for (j = 'A'; j <= 'C'; j++) {
            printf("%c%c ", i, j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: AA AB AC BA BB BC CA CB CC

m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loops iterate over char values ranging from A to C, printing each combination of i and j.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is printed by this code?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, sum = 0;
    for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        int j;
        for (j = i; j < 3; j++) {
            sum += 1;
        }
    }
    printf("%d", sum);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loop totals are (3-i) count: 3 + 2 + 1 = 6.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Find the output for the coded pattern:
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
            printf("%d", j);
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: It prints incremental numbers in each line up to the current row count.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Predict the output for a character pattern formed by nested loops.
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
            printf("# ");
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The code creates a triangle-like pattern of increasing number of # per line corresponding to the line number.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Find the total execution count for the inner print function.
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 2; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j <= 1; j++) {
            printf("Hello\n");
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loop design means that 'Hello' prints a combination of outer loops twice and inner twice, totaling 4.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be printed by the following nested loop code?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
        for (j = 3; j >= i; j--) {
            printf("$ ");
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Printing error due to loop construction

m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loop executes the inner loop for decreasing count, printing an inverted triangle of $.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Determine the final output of the following code with nested loops:
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 2; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j <= 2; j++) {
            if (i == j) continue;
            printf("%d%d ", i, j);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The output includes all pairs except where i equals j due to the continue condition.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the purpose of nesting loops in the following example?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j, product;
    for (i = 1; i <= 2; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= 2; j++) {
            product = i * j;
            printf("%d ", product);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: To print a multiplication table for 2x2
To create a sequence of even numbers
To print odd numbered products only

m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loops iterate over a 2x2 range multiplying indices, fulfilling a basic multiplication table.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the final printed string from this nested loop program?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 3; i > 0; i--) {
        for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
            printf("@ ");
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The nested loop creates an inverted triangle pattern, decreasing the length of '@ ' output each line.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Why does this C nested loop form a square instead of other regular shapes?
c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    int i, j;
    for (i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
        for (j = 1; j <= 4; j++) {
            printf("*");
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Outer and inner loops iterate equally
The code should use separate printf for each line

m_Item1.Explanation: Both the outer and inner loops iterate over the exact range, creating a consistent grid or square pattern.
